primary.title: Self-Evolving LLM Memory Extraction Across Heterogeneous Tasks
primary.abstract: As LLM-based assistants become persistent and personalized, they must extract and retain useful information from past conversations as memory. However, the types of information worth remembering vary considerably across tasks. We formalize the heterogeneous memory extraction task and introduce BEHEMOTH, a benchmark that repurposes 18 existing datasets spanning personalization, problem-solving, and agentic tasks, using a downstream utility-driven metric for systematic evaluation. Our empirical analysis confirms that no single static extraction prompt dominates across all task categories, and that existing self-evolving prompt optimization frameworks, originally designed for homogeneous distributions, degrade when training tasks are heterogeneous. To address this, we propose CluE, a cluster-based self-evolving strategy that groups training examples into clusters by extraction scenarios, analyzes each cluster independently, and synthesizes cross-cluster insights to update the extraction prompt. Experiments on BEHEMOTH show that CluE generalizes effectively across heterogeneous tasks (+9.04\% relative gain), consistently outperforming prior self-evolving frameworks.

paper.ai_summary: LLM-based assistants require heterogeneous memory extraction capabilities, which are evaluated through the BEHEMOTH benchmark, with CluE offering improved performance through cluster-based prompt optimization.

raw.summary: As LLM-based assistants become persistent and personalized, they must extract and retain useful information from past conversations as memory. However, the types of information worth remembering vary considerably across tasks. We formalize the heterogeneous memory extraction task and introduce BEHEMOTH, a benchmark that repurposes 18 existing datasets spanning personalization, problem-solving, and agentic tasks, using a downstream utility-driven metric for systematic evaluation. extra.paper_digest:
No benchmark exists for evaluating memory extraction systems across heterogeneous tasks, and static extraction prompts degrade when training and testing tasks differ.

The paper introduces BEHEMOTH, a benchmark repurposing 18 datasets with a downstream utility-driven metric, and proposes CluE, a cluster-based self-evolving strategy that groups extraction scenarios and synthesizes cross-cluster insights.

- Using memory consistently improves over no-memory baselines; even the minimal Simpleprompt raises overall macro accuracy from 37.84% to 46.00%

- Experiments on BEHEMOTH show that CluE generalizes effectively across heterogeneous tasks ( +9.04% relative gain), consistently outperforming prior self-evolving frameworks.
- Despite this challenge, there is no established benchmark for evaluating the generalizability of memory extraction systemsacross heterogeneous tasks.
- • Through evaluation on BEHEMOTH, we reveal that no single static prompt dominates across all categories, and that existing self-evolving frameworks struggle to generalize across heterogeneous tasks.
- Both lines of work rely on fixed, domain-specific extraction rules, limiting their generalizability.

competitors.title: Self-Evolving LLM Memory Extraction Across Heterogeneous Tasks
competitors.abstract: As LLM-based assistants become persistent and personalized, they must extract retain useful information from past conversations as memory. However, the types of worth remembering vary considerably across tasks. We formalize \textit{heterogeneous memory extraction} task introduce \textbf{BEHEMOTH}, a benchmark that repurposes 18 existing datasets spanning personalization, problem-solving, agentic tasks, using downstream utility-driven metric for systematic evaluation. Our empirical analysis confirms no single static extraction prompt dominates all categories, self-evolving optimization frameworks, originally designed homogeneous distributions, degrade when training tasks are heterogeneous. To address this, we propose \textbf{CluE}, cluster-based strategy groups examples into clusters by scenarios, analyzes each cluster independently, synthesizes cross-cluster insights to update prompt. Experiments on BEHEMOTH show CluE generalizes effectively heterogeneous ($+$9.04\% relative gain), consistently outperforming prior frameworks.

competitors.title: A Survey of Large Language Models
competitors.abstract: Language is essentially a complex, intricate system of human expressions governed by grammatical rules. It poses significant challenge to develop capable AI algorithms for comprehending and grasping language. As major approach, language modeling has been widely studied understanding generation in the past two decades, evolving from statistical models neural models. Recently, pre-trained (PLMs) have proposed pre-training Transformer over large-scale corpora, showing strong capabilities solving various NLP tasks. Since researchers found that model scaling can lead performance improvement, they further study effect increasing size an even larger size. Interestingly, when parameter scale exceeds certain level, these enlarged not only achieve improvement but also show some special abilities are present small-scale To discriminate difference scale, research community coined term large (LLM) PLMs on LLMs largely advanced both academia industry, remarkable progress launch ChatGPT, which attracted widespread attention society. The technical evolution making important impact entire community, would revolutionize way how we use algorithms. In this survey, review recent advances introducing background, key findings, mainstream techniques. particular, focus four aspects LLMs, namely pre-training, adaptation tuning, utilization, capacity evaluation. Besides, summarize available resources developing discuss remaining issues future directions.

competitors.title: Quantifying the advantage of domain-specific pre-training on named entity recognition tasks in materials science
competitors.abstract: A bottleneck in efficiently connecting new materials discoveries to established literature has arisen due an increase publications. This problem may be addressed by using named entity recognition (NER) extract structured summary-level data from unstructured science text. We compare the performance of four NER models on three datasets. The include a bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) and transformer (BERT, SciBERT, MatBERT) with increasing degrees domain-specific pre-training. MatBERT improves over other two BERT<sub>BASE</sub>-based 1%∼12%, implying that pre-training provides measurable advantages. Despite relative architectural simplicity, BiLSTM model consistently outperforms BERT, perhaps its pre-trained word embeddings. Furthermore, SciBERT outperform original BERT greater extent small limit. MatBERT's higher-quality predictions should accelerate extraction literature.

section_evidence.paper_problem: No benchmark exists for evaluating memory extraction systems across heterogeneous tasks, and static extraction prompts degrade when training and testing tasks differ.
section_evidence.paper_method: The paper introduces BEHEMOTH, a benchmark repurposing 18 datasets with a downstream utility-driven metric, and proposes CluE, a cluster-based self-evolving strategy that groups extraction scenarios and synthesizes cross-cluster insights.
section_evidence.paper_setup: The evaluation uses BEHEMOTH, a benchmark comprising 18 datasets across three task categories. Existing self‑evolving methods (GEPA, ACE, MemEvolve) are tested under this heterogeneous setting.
section_evidence.paper_experiments: - Using memory consistently improves over no-memory baselines; even the minimal Simpleprompt raises overall macro accuracy from 37.84% to 46.00%
section_evidence.paper_limitations:
- Experiments on BEHEMOTH show that CluE generalizes effectively across heterogeneous tasks ( +9.04% relative gain), consistently outperforming prior self-evolving frameworks.
- Despite this challenge, there is no established benchmark for evaluating the generalizability of memory extraction systemsacross heterogeneous tasks.
- • Through evaluation on BEHEMOTH, we reveal that no single static prompt dominates across all categories, and that existing self-evolving frameworks struggle to generalize across heterogeneous tasks.
- Both lines of work rely on fixed, domain-specific extraction rules, limiting their generalizability.
section_evidence.paper_conclusion: In this work, we focus on LLM memory extraction under heterogeneous tasks. We formalize single-step memory extraction, introduce a general benchmark methodology instantiated as BEHEMOTH, and use it to expose the limitations of both static extraction prompts and existing self-evolving frameworks. To overcome these limitations, we propose CluE, a cluster-based evolution framework that enables stable learning under heterogeneous training distributions, and demonstrate its effectiveness through extensive experiments. We believe both artifacts of this work may find use beyond the scope studied here. BEHE- MOTH can serve as a testbed for evaluating a wide range of memory extraction approaches— not only self-evolving frameworks but also routing-based, skill-based, and other emerging paradigms—to explore what strategies best handle heterogeneous memory extraction. The self-evolving framework CluE may also extend beyond memory extraction to any setting where a single agent must handle heterogen
section_evidence.paper_reproducibility: - Uses two evaluation metrics: Macro Accuracy (equal-weighted average across datasets) and Relative Gain (geometric mean of per-dataset improvement ratios over baseline)

Difficulty in evaluating the true 'self-evolving' capability without implementation details
Experiments on BEHEMOTH show that CluE generalizes effectively across heterogeneous tasks ( +9.04% relative gain), consistently outperforming prior self-evolving frameworks.
Despite this challenge, there is no established benchmark for evaluating the generalizability of memory extraction systemsacross heterogeneous tasks.
• Through evaluation on BEHEMOTH, we reveal that no single static prompt dominates across all categories, and that existing self-evolving frameworks struggle to generalize across heterogeneous tasks.
Both lines of work rely on fixed, domain-specific extraction rules, limiting their generalizability.

3.2 Creating BEHEMOTH

As existing memory benchmarks cover only homogeneous task distributions, we provide a method to construct aheterogeneousbenchmark that covers diverse tasks under a unified protocol, enabling the first systematic study of memory extraction generalizability.

committee_synthesis:
The paper introduces a self‑evolving LLM architecture that extracts and refines task‑specific memories on the fly, then reuses those memories across a wide variety of downstream problems. By repurposing 18 existing datasets into the BEHEMOTH benchmark and measuring performance with a macro‑accuracy utility metric, the authors show that even a lightweight memory module (the Simpleprompt variant) lifts baseline performance from 37.84 % to 46.00 % macro accuracy. The more sophisticated CluE strategy builds on this foundation: it clusters extraction scenarios, synthesizes insights across clusters, and continuously updates the memory bank, yielding an additional relative gain of roughly 9 % over the simple baseline.

The central tension lies in how to make memory both stable and adaptable. On one hand, the system must preserve previously extracted knowledge so that it does not catastrophically forget when faced with new tasks; on the other hand, it must evolve its extraction rules to capture task‑specific patterns that improve generalization. The authors acknowledge that existing self‑evolving frameworks often rely on fixed, domain‑specific extraction rules, which limits their ability to transfer across heterogeneous domains. Moreover, evaluating the true “self‑evolving” nature of the approach remains challenging without detailed implementation traces, and there is an open question about the computational overhead incurred by constantly clustering and updating memory structures.

For readers, the key takeaway is that augmenting LLMs with a self‑evolving memory mechanism can deliver measurable, cross‑task performance improvements—demonstrated by consistent macro‑accuracy gains on the BEHEMOTH benchmark—while also highlighting critical design challenges. Future work should focus on balancing knowledge retention with adaptive rule evolution, reducing the computational cost of memory updates, and developing more rigorous evaluation protocols that can verify the continual self‑evolution of the memory system in realistic, unseen‑task scenarios.
